title: International trade and comparative advantage
summary: Why do nations that could make everything themselves still choose to trade? This HL lesson builds the answer from the ground up: the benefits of international trade, Adam Smith's absolute advantage, and David Ricardo's comparative advantage based on lower opportunity cost. You will learn to read a two-country, two-good table, calculate opportunity-cost ratios, pin down which country should specialise in what, prove the gains from trade numerically, and state the assumptions and limitations of the theory. Because 4.1 is assessed on Paper 3, the focus throughout is on accurate, examinable calculation.
learningObjectives: Explain the benefits of international trade and distinguish absolute advantage from comparative advantage.
Calculate opportunity-cost ratios from an output (or input) table to determine which country has the comparative advantage in each good.
Show, with numbers, how specialisation according to comparative advantage and trade at mutually beneficial terms of trade produces gains for both countries.
State and evaluate the assumptions and limitations of the theory of comparative advantage.
levelNote: HL only — Higher Level content assessed on Paper 3.

simpleExplanation.title: Do What You Give Up Least To Do
simpleExplanation.summary: Even a country that is better at making everything cannot make everything at the same time — producing more of one good always means giving up some of another. Comparative advantage says each country should focus on the good it sacrifices the LEAST to make, then trade for the rest. Do this and both countries end up able to consume more than they could alone.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a champion chef who is also the fastest dishwasher in town. She is absolutely better at both jobs. But every hour she spends washing dishes is an hour she is not cooking the meals that earn the restaurant a fortune. A trainee washes dishes more slowly, yet gives up almost nothing valuable by doing so. The smart split is clear: the chef cooks, the trainee washes, and the restaurant serves more customers than if the chef tried to do both. The chef's ADVANTAGE is absolute, but the sensible division of labour follows opportunity cost — which is exactly comparative advantage.
steps: Start from the two countries' maximum outputs of two goods with no trade (autarky).
Turn outputs into opportunity-cost ratios: how much of the other good is given up for one unit of each good.
The country with the LOWER opportunity cost in a good has the comparative advantage and should specialise in it.
Trade at a terms of trade between the two opportunity costs, and both countries consume beyond their own production frontier.

flashcards.back: The ability of a country to produce more of a good than another country using the same resources (or the same output with fewer resources). Introduced by Adam Smith. It compares physical productivity, NOT opportunity cost.

The ability of a country to produce a good at a LOWER opportunity cost than another country. Introduced by David Ricardo. This — not absolute advantage — is the basis for beneficial specialisation and trade.

The amount of the OTHER good given up to produce one unit of a good. From an output table, the opportunity cost of 1 unit of Good A = (output of Good B) / (output of Good A), measured in units of Good B.

The extra combined consumption available when each country specialises in its lower-opportunity-cost good and trades. World output rises and both partners can consume outside their own production possibilities.

flashcards.front: Terms of trade (for gains to exist)
flashcards.back: The exchange ratio at which the two goods are traded. For BOTH countries to gain, the terms of trade must lie strictly between the two countries' opportunity costs for the traded good.
Concentrating a country's resources on producing the good in which it has a comparative advantage, rather than trying to produce everything domestically.
Economic self-sufficiency — no international trade. Under autarky a country's consumption is confined to its own production possibilities curve.
Through specialisation and trade a country can CONSUME a bundle outside its own PPC. It does not produce beyond its PPC; its consumption possibilities frontier extends past its production frontier.

flashcards.front: Can a country have a comparative advantage in BOTH goods?
flashcards.back: No. Opportunity costs are reciprocals: a higher opportunity cost in one good mathematically forces a lower opportunity cost in the other. If a country has the comparative advantage in one good, its partner has it in the other.

An OUTPUT table lists how much each country can make with its resources — divide the OTHER good by the good in question. An INPUT table lists resources (e.g. labour hours) needed per unit — here you compare cost RATIOS, and the good needing relatively fewer resources is the comparative-advantage good. Read the axis labels before calculating.

flashcards.front: When do gains from trade disappear?
flashcards.back: If the two countries have the SAME opportunity-cost ratios (identical relative costs), no comparative advantage exists and specialisation yields no gains. Gains require DIFFERENT opportunity costs.

quickCheck.prompt: A country that can produce MORE of every good than its trading partner, using the same resources, has:
options: A comparative advantage in every good
An absolute advantage in every good
No reason to trade with anyone
A lower opportunity cost in every good
quickCheck.answer: An absolute advantage in every good

quickCheck.prompt: Comparative advantage in a good belongs to the country that produces it at the:
From an OUTPUT table, the opportunity cost of 1 unit of cloth for a country is calculated as:
Country A gives up 3 wheat per cloth; Country B gives up 5 wheat per cloth. Which country should specialise in cloth, and why?
options: B, because it produces more wheat
A, because it has the lower opportunity cost in cloth
B, because it has an absolute advantage
Neither — the opportunity costs are too similar
quickCheck.answer: A, because it has the lower opportunity cost in cloth

quickCheck.prompt: For a mutually beneficial trade in cars where the opportunity cost of 1 car is 2 wheat in one country and 6 wheat in the other, the agreed terms of trade must be:

faq.q: If a country is worse at producing everything, why would anyone trade with it?
faq.a: Because comparative advantage depends on relative, not absolute, efficiency. Even a country with an absolute disadvantage in both goods must have a LOWER opportunity cost in one of them — that is a mathematical certainty when opportunity costs differ. It specialises there and trades, and its partner, freed to concentrate on the good it sacrifices least to make, also gains. Both end up consuming more than in autarky, regardless of who is 'better' overall.

faq.q: What are the main assumptions and limitations of the theory?
faq.a: The Ricardian model assumes: only two countries and two goods; no transport costs; no trade barriers such as tariffs or quotas; constant opportunity costs (a straight-line PPC, so no economies or diseconomies of scale); perfect factor mobility so resources switch costlessly between industries; homogeneous products; and full information. In reality transport costs and protectionism erode gains, switching production causes structural unemployment, some industries have increasing returns, exchange-rate movements can overturn a paper advantage, and over-specialisation leaves a country exposed to demand or supply shocks in a single sector.

faq.q: Does specialising according to comparative advantage carry any risks?
faq.a: Yes. A country that specialises heavily may become dangerously dependent on one or two exports. If world demand or the price of that good collapses, or a poor harvest or supply shock hits, export revenue falls sharply with few alternatives to fall back on. Narrow specialisation can also lock developing economies into low-value primary products and expose workers in declining industries to unemployment when patterns of advantage shift. The theory shows gains are possible; it does not guarantee they are evenly shared or free of adjustment costs.

sections.content: Nations that could, in principle, make almost anything for themselves still trade enormous quantities across borders every day. Understanding why is one of the oldest and most powerful results in economics. In this HL lesson we build the case for trade from first principles — from Adam Smith's absolute advantage to David Ricardo's deeper insight of comparative advantage — and we do it the way Paper 3 demands: with tables, opportunity-cost ratios and worked numbers. By the end you will be able to look at a two-country table and say, with a calculation to back it up, exactly who should produce what and why both countries end up richer.
Why countries trade: the benefits

International trade lets a country consume goods it cannot produce at all, or cannot produce cheaply, and lets it sell what it makes efficiently to a much larger world market. The headline benefits are a wider choice of goods and services for consumers, lower prices through access to cheaper foreign production, larger markets that allow firms to exploit economies of scale, greater competition that drives efficiency and innovation, and access to resources and technology unavailable at home. Underneath all of these lies one engine: countries can specialise. The task of this lesson is to show precisely which good each country should specialise in, and the answer is not the obvious one.
Absolute advantage vs comparative advantage

Adam Smith described absolute advantage: a country has an absolute advantage in a good when it can produce more of it than another country from the same resources. If, with one unit of resources, Country X makes 60 units of wheat while Country Y makes 40, then X has the absolute advantage in wheat. Absolute advantage compares raw productivity — who can physically make more.

David Ricardo saw that absolute advantage cannot be the whole story, because a country might be absolutely better at producing everything, yet it still cannot produce everything at once. Making more of one good always means giving up some of another. Ricardo's comparative advantage compares those sacrifices: a country has a comparative advantage in the good it gives up the LEAST of the other good to produce — that is, the good in which it has the lower opportunity cost. It is comparative advantage, not absolute advantage, that determines the beneficial pattern of specialisation and trade.

items: **Absolute advantage** = who can produce MORE of a good with given resources (a comparison of output).
**Comparative advantage** = who produces a good at the LOWER opportunity cost (a comparison of sacrifices).
A country can have an absolute advantage in both goods, but it can NEVER have a comparative advantage in both — the opportunity costs are reciprocals.
Specialisation and the gains from trade follow COMPARATIVE, not absolute, advantage.

sections.content: Calculating opportunity-cost ratios from an output table

Paper 3 almost always gives you a table of maximum outputs and asks you to determine comparative advantage. The method is mechanical once you see it. If a country can make a maximum of either A units of Good A or B units of Good B with all its resources, then giving up all of Good B buys you A units of Good A — so one unit of Good A costs B/A units of Good B in forgone output.
\text{Opportunity cost of 1 unit of Good A} = \frac{\text{maximum output of Good B}}{\text{maximum output of Good A}} \quad(\text{measured in units of Good B})

The two opportunity costs for a country are reciprocals of one another: if 1 unit of A costs 2 units of B, then 1 unit of B costs 0.5 units of A. Compute the opportunity cost of a given good for BOTH countries, then whichever country's number is lower has the comparative advantage in that good. A quick caution on table type: an OUTPUT table (units produced) uses the division above; an INPUT table (resources such as labour hours needed per unit) is read the opposite way round — the good requiring relatively fewer resources reveals the comparative advantage. Always check the axis labels before you divide.
Worked example — determining comparative advantage

sections.question: Country X can produce 60 units of wheat OR 30 units of cloth. Country Y can produce 40 units of wheat OR 40 units of cloth. Using opportunity-cost ratios, determine which country has the comparative advantage in cloth. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**

**Step 1 — Opportunity cost of 1 cloth in Country X.**
X gives up 60 wheat to make 30 cloth, so the opportunity cost of 1 cloth = 60 ÷ 30 = **2 wheat**.

**Step 2 — Opportunity cost of 1 cloth in Country Y.**
Y gives up 40 wheat to make 40 cloth, so the opportunity cost of 1 cloth = 40 ÷ 40 = **1 wheat**.

**Step 3 — Compare.**
Y sacrifices only 1 wheat per cloth, whereas X sacrifices 2 wheat per cloth. Y gives up less.

**Conclusion:** Because Country Y has the LOWER opportunity cost of producing cloth (1 wheat < 2 wheat), **Country Y has the comparative advantage in cloth**. (It follows that X has the comparative advantage in wheat: 1 wheat costs X only 0.5 cloth, but costs Y a full 1 cloth.)

---
**How our marking engine awards the marks [4]:**
This is a Paper 3 quantitative question, so marks are split into METHOD (M) marks for the correct opportunity-cost approach and ACCURACY (A) marks for correct figures and conclusion, with working shown.
- **M1** — Correct opportunity-cost METHOD applied to Country X: dividing wheat by cloth (60 ÷ 30) to cost cloth in terms of wheat.
- **M1** — Correct opportunity-cost METHOD applied to Country Y: the same division (40 ÷ 40).
- **A1** — Correct RATIOS stated with units: X = 2 wheat per cloth, Y = 1 wheat per cloth.
- **A1** — Correct CONCLUSION consistent with the ratios: Y has the comparative advantage in cloth because its opportunity cost is lower.

**Follow-through (own-figure rule):** if a candidate mis-divides but then reasons correctly from their own numbers — for example, they compute the ratios the wrong way up but still select the country with the genuinely lower opportunity cost by their own working — the method and conclusion marks can still be awarded. The engine rewards a correct DECISION rule applied to the candidate's own figures, so showing every step protects marks even after an arithmetic slip. Naked answers with no working (a bare 'Y') cannot earn the M marks even when the letter is right.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button below does: attempt this [4]-mark question in your own words, submit it, and our marking engine grades it against these same M/A points — telling you which marks you secured and, crucially, whether your working would have earned follow-through after a slip. The Paper 3 habit that wins marks: SHOW every division and always attach units (2 wheat, not just 2).
From comparative advantage to the gains from trade

Once each country specialises in its lower-opportunity-cost good, total world output of both goods can rise, and trade lets each country reach a consumption bundle that its own resources could never produce. The bridge between the two countries is the terms of trade — the ratio at which the goods actually exchange. For BOTH sides to gain, that ratio must sit strictly between the two countries' opportunity costs; only then does each country get the imported good more cheaply through trade than it could make it at home.

sections.question: Return to Country X (60 wheat OR 30 cloth) and Country Y (40 wheat OR 40 cloth). X specialises in wheat and Y specialises in cloth. They agree to trade at 1.5 wheat per cloth, and Y exports 12 cloth to X. Show that both countries gain from trade. [6]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — Confirm the terms of trade lie between the opportunity costs.**
Opportunity cost of 1 cloth is 2 wheat in X and 1 wheat in Y. The agreed rate, 1.5 wheat per cloth, satisfies 1 < 1.5 < 2, so it can benefit both.

**Step 2 — Specialised production.**
X produces 60 wheat, 0 cloth. Y produces 40 cloth, 0 wheat.

**Step 3 — Trade: Y exports 12 cloth to X at 1.5 wheat per cloth.**
X pays 12 × 1.5 = 18 wheat for the 12 cloth.
- **X consumes:** 60 − 18 = **42 wheat and 12 cloth**.
- **Y consumes:** 40 − 12 = 28 cloth, plus 18 wheat received = **18 wheat and 28 cloth**.

**Step 4 — Compare each to what it could produce alone (its own PPC).**
- **Country X:** To have 12 cloth in autarky, X must give up 12 × 2 = 24 wheat, leaving 60 − 24 = 36 wheat. Through trade X has 12 cloth AND 42 wheat — that is **6 more wheat** than autarky for the same 12 cloth. Gain confirmed.
- **Country Y:** To have 18 wheat in autarky, Y must give up 18 × 1 = 18 cloth, leaving 40 − 18 = 22 cloth. Through trade Y has 18 wheat AND 28 cloth — that is **6 more cloth** than autarky for the same 18 wheat. Gain confirmed.

**Conclusion:** Both countries consume a bundle lying OUTSIDE their own production possibilities frontier, so specialisation and trade at 1.5 wheat per cloth make both better off than autarky.

sections.content: To 'show gains from trade' you must COMPARE the post-trade bundle with what the country could have made itself — do not just state the final consumption. The cleanest proof holds one good constant and shows the country ends up with more of the other (here, +6 wheat for X and +6 cloth for Y). On a diagram, draw the PPC and the consumption possibilities line running out from the point of specialisation at the terms-of-trade slope, and mark a consumption point beyond the PPC.
Assumptions and limitations of the theory
The elegance of comparative advantage rests on strong assumptions. Knowing them lets you evaluate the model, which is where higher-band Paper 3 and essay marks are won.

items: **No transport costs** — real shipping and logistics costs can wipe out a small comparative advantage.
**No trade barriers** — tariffs, quotas and subsidies distort or block the specialisation the model predicts.
**Constant opportunity costs** — the simple model assumes a straight-line PPC; with economies or diseconomies of scale the ratios shift as output changes.
**Perfect factor mobility** — resources are assumed to switch costlessly between industries, but in reality workers face retraining and structural unemployment when a country reallocates production.
**Homogeneous products and full information** — real goods are differentiated by brand and quality, and buyers and sellers lack perfect knowledge.
**Only two countries and two goods** — the classroom model is a simplification of a many-country, many-good world.
**Risks of over-specialisation** — a country concentrated in one or two exports is exposed to price collapses, demand shifts or supply shocks in that sector.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Choosing specialisation by absolute advantage** — deciding who produces what by who makes MORE, rather than by lower opportunity cost. The whole point of Ricardo is that these can differ.
**Claiming a country has a comparative advantage in both goods** — impossible, because opportunity costs are reciprocals; if you calculate this, you have made an arithmetic error.
**Dividing the wrong way in an output table** — the opportunity cost of a good is the OTHER good's output divided by that good's output. Mixing up numerator and denominator flips the answer.
**Confusing output tables with input tables** — an input (labour-hours) table is read in reverse; treating it like an output table inverts the conclusion.
**Omitting units** — writing '2' instead of '2 wheat per cloth' costs accuracy marks on Paper 3.
**Setting terms of trade outside the opportunity-cost range** — a rate at or beyond one country's opportunity cost leaves that country no better than autarky, so trade is not mutually beneficial.
**Saying trade lets a country PRODUCE beyond its PPC** — trade extends CONSUMPTION beyond the PPC, not production.
**Stating a final bundle without proving the gain** — you must compare to the autarky alternative to demonstrate the gain from trade.

sections.content: How this maps to Paper 3 marking

Paper 3 quantitative questions are marked on POINTS: METHOD marks (M) for setting up the correct calculation, ACCURACY marks (A) for the right figures and a conclusion consistent with them, marks for showing working, and follow-through so that a correct method applied to your own (even slightly wrong) earlier figure is still rewarded. The practical lesson is simple: never write a bare answer. Lay out each opportunity-cost division, keep the units attached, and state a conclusion that follows from your numbers. That is how you capture every M and A mark available — and how the marking engine below evaluates your response.

sections.label: Get a Paper 3 comparative-advantage calculation marked: determine comparative advantage and prove the gains from trade
